project_overview.project_development_objective: To support the recovery of livelihoods and infrastructure in flood and drought affected areas and strengthen capacity for disaster preparedness nationwide
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 300,000 households (1.6 million persons on 5:1 ratio), including focus on female-headed households, nearly 160,000 livestock, and rehabilitated assets for over 40 communities

geographic_scope.coverage_type: Regional (three flood-affected states) with national approach to locust response and disaster preparedness
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes - partial. Somalia is in active conflict with Al Shabaab insurgency, particularly affecting the three flood-affected states. More than 60 warring parties present. Government access beyond urban areas restricted. Project locations in government-influenced areas only.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Immediate Basic Services and Livelihood Support
key_components.description: Cash-for-work schemes for vulnerable households; desert locust control through surveillance and spraying; agricultural inputs provision; livestock disease prevention; basic health services and GBV support; household hygiene promotion

Rehabilitation of critical infrastructure including roads, WASH systems, flood risk management infrastructure, irrigation schemes, and health facilities to build-back-better and climate-resilient standards

Integrated flood-drought preparedness solutions; flood risk management strengthening; hydromet and early warning systems; operationalization of National Drought Recovery and Resilience Framework; National Emergency Operations Centre; public-civil society collaboration models; locust early warning; disease surveillance and laboratory capacity; health facility rehabilitation

Strengthening PIU and State Project Teams capacity for implementation, procurement, financial management, M&E, environmental and social safeguards compliance, grievance redress, and GBV/SEA protection
Flexible financing for response to natural or man-made disasters and major disease outbreaks during project life; can be mobilized for early response to food security crises or disease outbreaks

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Government of Somalia (FGS) through Ministry of Finance (MOF) and Ministry of Planning, Investment and Economic Development (MoPIED); Federal Member States (FMS) through their respective MDAs and State Project Teams
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by OPM with DGs from FGS and FMS; Project Implementation Unit (PIU) in MOF co-managed with MoPIED; State Project Teams (SPT) in each FMS; coordination with UN agencies (FAO, UNOPS, WHO, UNICEF), INGO consortia, and development partners; linkages to National Emergency Operations Centre (NEOC) and Recovery and Resilience Framework (RRF) Secretariat
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank New Procurement Framework; Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD); advance contracting and retroactive financing permitted; UNOPS and FAO contracted as service providers; INGO consortia for early recovery; STEP system for tracking; special procurement arrangements for FCV context
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PIU responsible for overall M&E using Results Framework; quarterly updates on implementation; data collection system for baseline and periodic surveys; third-party independent verification agents (IVA) for objective feedback every 6 months; Management Information System (MIS) for continuous tracking; Community Scorecard exercises; citizen engagement in monitoring; M&E Community of Practice established

identified_risks.risk_description: Political turmoil from contested FGS/FMS relations; upcoming elections (late 2020/early 2021) could heighten tensions; competition over scarce financial resources; clan-based grievances and tensions; weak political commitment to partnerships; low transparency and accountability; corruption perceptions index ranks Somalia at bottom (9/100); absence of effective accountability mechanisms
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project Steering Committee with FGS/FMS representation; transparent communication on selection modalities and sequencing; regular stakeholder engagement; UNOPS support for contested political space; conflict-sensitive project design; local area partnerships through INGO consortia; federal model allowing regional prioritization; OPM high-level oversight

identified_risks.risk_description: Somalia is one of world's poorest countries (GNI US$284); 80% of exports from single product (livestock); exceptional vulnerability to external shocks; substantial donor funding dependency; fiscal fragility and weak revenue base; potential for drought, livestock bans, commercial bank de-risking, remittance flow changes, disease outbreaks, or export bans to wipe out revenues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Flexible project design enabling activity adjustment to changing conditions; robust locust response to prevent food insecurity; World Bank and IMF engagement in strengthening macroeconomic framework

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak sector policies and no overall sector framework; sector strategies not fully funded with uncertain future funding; risk of development support failing to materialize; challenge of transitioning from humanitarian to development response; potential for humanitarian challenges to draw focus from development actions; insufficient coordination between World Bank projects
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Sustained World Bank engagement at critical stage; development approaches to reduce vulnerabilities and build resilience; capacity support for humanitarian-development nexus; regular communication between related project teams; potential joint resource utilization across PIUs

identified_risks.risk_description: Minimal complexity for Somalia context but still challenging; PIU limited experience with small-scale projects only; risk of unrealistic expectations for simultaneous delivery across locations before capacity generated; volatile security environment preventing swift hiring of qualified professionals; delays in procurement and consultant recruitment; low quality technical designs; weak capacity for technical design, procurement, construction supervision
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Minimal complexity project design with five components and sequenced implementation; focus on local labor and labor-intensive technologies; off-the-shelf designs where possible; capacity building for ESF and M&E; immediate PIU staff hiring building on SFF-LD experience; assessment of operational environment; clear communication strategy; area-based approaches; extensive consultations at federal, regional, local levels; only contractors with proven Somalia track record; technical assistance for capacity building

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Very low FGS and FMS capacity; no coherent rules for day-to-day operations; PIU among strongest FGS units but many positions unfilled, key procedures untested, no track record on robust M&E; limited WB experience; core ESF policies not previously applied; risk of PIU staff reassignment to other government parts; significant gaps in M&E and ESF arrangements
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Significant PIU strengthening through financial and administrative competence building; external consultants for design and implementation; engagement of reputable service providers; substantial capacity building resource allocation; close exchange with other WB projects; assessment of private sector capacities; dedicated GBV expert in PIU; training on WB FM procedures; on-the-job training and skills acquisition through UNOPS

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk of funds not achieving value-for-money with integrity; lack of key FM competencies; weak internal controls and oversight; rudimentary accounting and reporting systems; over-reliance on external technical assistance; non-existent banking arrangements; inadequate internal and external audit; lack of public procurement guidelines and regulations; high fraud and corruption potential; absence of robust public financial legal framework; nascent rudimentary banking systems; risks of late document submission, poor filing, non-compliance to work plans, unauthorized supplier commitments, bypassing oversight, high cash handling risks in dollarized economy
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Integration of FM function into country systems; External Assistance Fiduciary Section (EAFS) oversight; dedicated project accountant in PIU; periodic training on Bank FM procedures; detailed FM procedures in POM; strong FM staffing arrangements; periodic FM supervision missions; stringent reviews and monitoring; clear asset ownership guidelines; social accountability and transparency measures; specific corruption auditing in external audit ToR; independent monitoring and risk management mechanism; UNOPS and FAO FM frameworks; qualified CSO assessment; anti-corruption guidelines compliance

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental and social risks. Environmental: unknown specific project locations; very low environmental baseline; potential risk from locust chemical spraying to environment, communities, control teams; risks to environmental services, waste management, pollution control, topsoil, vegetation, drainage, erosion, sedimentation, biodiversity, human health and safety. Social: perception of systematic bias in fragile environment; Somalia fragmented along social cleavages; real or perceived marginalization could cause severe repercussions; competition during tender processes; high GBV risk country; risk of marginalized communities, minorities, women excluded from consultations or participation; restricted access to some areas; security challenges for project operations; exclusion of vulnerable groups; selection bias and elite capture; difficulty accessing beneficiaries for monitoring; inward migration risks; SEA/H and GBV heightened through project activities; systemic weakness in PIU capacity to identify and prevent adverse social impacts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ESMF preparation and disclosure before activities commence; Pest Management Plan (PMP) for locust control aligned with FAO Directives; site-specific ESIAs/ESMPs for higher risk projects; subproject screening to exclude sensitive areas; capacity assessment and building measures; Health Care Waste Management Plan; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); Resettlement Plan Framework (RPF); GBV Action Plan; Security Management Plan (SMP); Labor Management Procedures (LMP); dedicated environmental and social safeguards specialist in PIU; dedicated GBV specialist; GBV service provider contracting; safety audits for all relevant activities; GBV referral system in three flood-affected states; regular GBV risk assessment and management; community engagement processes; transparent and inclusive community engagement; effective project communication; Labor Management Procedures with code of conduct; GRM with SEA/H and GBV sensitive channels; survivor-centered care protocols; capacity gap identification and institutional capacity building; regular consultation with women and women's groups; do no harm principle; conflict-sensitive design; extensive consultations; communication strategy on marginalization; iterative adaptation mechanism through GRM

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk of project instrumentalization by political and clan actors; FMS may object to needs-based modality, geographical priorities, funds flow arrangements, phasing; regions may perceive disadvantage relative to other FMS; opposition from civil society, private sector, Al Shabaab, general public due to sectarian conflict nature; lack of understanding of project objectives
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong field-level INGO partnering; inclusive consultation, selection, validation process; monitoring by OPM; broad-based understanding of project modalities; transparent discussion of site selection, consultation processes, representation in committees; salary scheme transparency developed under Capacity Injection project; people-centered approach; use of trusted implementing partners; targeted communications strategy; clear project objectives

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from highly insecure operational environment; ongoing Al Shabaab insurgency particularly in three flood-affected states; despite AMISOM presence, active conflict may inhibit access to project sites and directly threaten project staff and beneficiaries; criminality as strong driver of insecurity; geographical focus on government influence areas may consolidate perceptions in other rural areas; insurgency definitive issue for access
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Agile and flexible implementation planning; regular security advice from FGS and UN; security decision-support mechanism producing security protocols and conflict monitoring; lessons from WB projects in NE Nigeria and Afghanistan; risk management approach ensuring sound stakeholder engagement; credible clan-based engagement; WB team monitoring of political and security environment with UN collaboration; meetings within Mogadishu International Airport and Nairobi; contracts to firms instead of individuals for security arrangements; emphasis on Somali staff; exploration of security firm services; WB team operation under corporate security guidance and UN umbrella; WB security agent hiring; security/logistics firm for in-country presence

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial reputational risk to World Bank from project outcomes; reputational risks for FGS particularly regarding INGO and UN agency implementation; potential for project failure to damage WB reputation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Risk mitigation through safeguards ensuring project success; MoU with government on key engagement principles (branding, communication); harmonized beneficiary registry/targeting methodology; ensuring FGS visibility in service delivery to citizens

identified_risks.risk_description: Climate-related security risks; interlinkages between climate change, cyclical drought, poverty, fragility, food insecurity, and protracted conflict; climate impacts on livelihood options and migration; accumulation of household debt as key vulnerability; grievances linked to group affiliation as serious risk factors affecting conflicts on multiple levels
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Integrated flood-drought response and preparedness regime; comprehensive longer-term interventions; conflict-sensitive project design; community engagement; government positioning and visibility; linkages to RRF; coordination with humanitarian-development nexus actors

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level GRM designed to address concerns and complaints promptly and transparently with no cost or discrimination. GRM includes SEA/H and GBV sensitive channels for reporting. Response and referral protocols for survivor-centered care. Documented records of stakeholder engagement and GRM maintained. Community Scorecard exercises for formal feedback. Beneficiaries can also access World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Dedicated consultations with women and female-headed households to identify unique needs. Differentiated approaches for livelihood and basic services. Strengthening GBV and child protection interventions with focus on referral pathways and service providers. Women's inclusion in decision-making structures including community grievance committees and preparedness planning. Gender balance in PIU recruitment. Gender-disaggregated indicators tracking female beneficiaries and female-headed households. Target of 40% of initiatives identified by females and 30% women representation in decision-making committees. 70% of females benefiting from referral pathways.

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict-sensitivity principles. Extensive consultations conducted with FGS and FMS to understand political economy dynamics and clan structures. Clan-based engagement critical to mitigating clan-based violence. Project uses 'do no harm' principle. Conflict-sensitive targeting framework to avoid elite capture and ensure inclusion of vulnerable groups. Community engagement processes designed to be gender and conflict-risk sensitive. INGO consortia with experience in conflict settings engaged. Project aims to strengthen state-citizen trust and legitimacy through visible government service delivery. Localized approach empowering FMS to be responsive to communities. Transparent and inclusive government-led prioritization to address perception of bias. Regular communication on project activities and allocations to sustain technical-level cooperation beyond political turbulence.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Somalia characterized by: (1) Protracted armed conflict with Al Shabaab insurgency and 60+ warring parties; (2) State collapse legacy with weak transitional governance; (3) Complex federal system with FMS power-sharing tensions; (4) Cyclical climate shocks (drought 2016-17, 2019; floods 2019); (5) Interlinkages between climate change, poverty, fragility, food insecurity, and conflict; (6) 2.6 million internally displaced persons; (7) High poverty (nearly 7 in 10 Somalis); (8) Weak institutional capacity and limited government access beyond urban areas; (9) Limited early warning and disaster management systems; (10) Nascent formal public institutions with limited human capacity and overlapping mandates; (11) Absence of livelihood options contributing to grievances; (12) Household debt accumulation as key vulnerability; (13) Limited resources against widespread need; (14) Weak rule of law and lawlessness in some areas; (15) Recent 2.6 million person displacement from conflict and climate shocks.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Somalia remains highly insecure operational environment. Al Shabaab insurgency particularly affects three flood-affected states (Hirshabelle, South West, Jubaland). Government access beyond urban areas and key towns restricted. More than 60 warring parties present. Drought and competition over resources fuel rural fighting. Project locations limited to government-influenced areas only. Security decision-support system to be implemented with security protocols and conflict monitoring. Standard operating procedures for when to suspend activities due to insecurity. Sequencing of activities can be revised through PSC to mitigate security risks. Contracts issued to firms rather than individuals for security arrangements. Emphasis on Somali staff to reduce international presence risks. WB team operates under corporate security guidance and UN umbrella. Security agent and logistics firm to support in-country presence. Meetings held in secure locations (Mogadishu International Airport, Nairobi). Regular security advice from FGS and UN informs implementation.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - significant displacement context. Project affects 2.6 million internally displaced persons (IDPs). Recent floods (late 2019) displaced 370,000 people from homes across 17 districts in 10 regions in three states. At least 154 IDP sites damaged by flooding. IDPs identified as vulnerable group requiring targeted support. Project includes activities to support IDP settlements and restore access to basic services. Female IDPs specifically noted as facing heightened risks including GBV. Project aims to support durable solutions to displacement through livelihood recovery and resilience building. Resettlement Plan Framework (RPF) prepared to address potential land acquisition and resettlement issues. Voluntary land donation carefully managed with robust due diligence. Formal mechanisms for land acquisition and eminent domain non-existent in most parts of Somalia, requiring careful management.
